# Questions in Livestream 32

Live Stream #32: Last Stream for 2021

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">5:21</a> | What advice would he give a medical doctor who wants to enter archaeology? | He said archaeology is much more research-driven than medical practice and usually requires moving on to a terminal research degree such as a PhD. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=475" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">7:55</a> | Is this really the last livestream of 2021? | Yes. He said he would not stream the next day, though Mr. Empty Conjecture might appear again. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:07</a> | Is nationhood a modern construct? | He said no. Ancient peoples had real national identities, even if the modern nation-state is a later form of that idea. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=667" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:07</a> | How were Yahweh's "three omnis" communicated to an ancient audience? | He pointed to creation, fulfilled prophecy, and divine judgment as ways scripture portrays God's power, knowledge, and goodness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=915" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:15</a> | What are his favorite anime and manga? | He named Mobile Suit Gundam, especially Thunderbolt and War in the Pocket, and said Your Lie in April was one of the best manga he had read recently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=1155" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:15</a> | How was human sacrifice practiced in Canaan? | He said it was typically child sacrifice, usually chosen by parents either as an appeal for prosperity or as a desperate act during siege conditions. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=1427" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:47</a> | Did ancient Egyptians view Canaanites and similar peoples as deeply wicked? | He said Egypt's view was strongly xenophobic: neighboring peoples were often treated as chaotic, inferior, and culturally lesser, though not always described in exactly biblical moral terms. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=1662" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">27:42</a> | What advice would he give a new Christian beginning theology studies in college? | He urged studying theology as an act of worship so it does not become spiritually dry or merely mechanical. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=1767" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:27</a> | What did he think of the Jonathan Sheffield and C. J. Cox versus Josh Bowen and Maggie Bryson debate? | He thought all four did their best, but said the debate was disorganized, lacked subject-matter expertise, and did not get to the key Exodus issues. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=1992" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:12</a> | What minimum level of technology was needed to build a pyramid? | He said not much by modern standards: bronze tools, sledges, boats, and practical quarrying methods were enough, even without wheels or common iron use. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=2278" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">37:58</a> | Do we find Egyptian pottery when Israel took over in Canaan, and if not, why not? | He said not much outside garrison contexts. Egypt mainly used Canaan to extract goods, so local pottery traditions usually remained dominant. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=2434" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:34</a> | When do specifically Israelite artifacts begin appearing in Canaan? | He said the earliest clear material is mostly pottery near the end of the Late Bronze Age, with the transition from Canaanite forms appearing gradually rather than all at once. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=2669" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:29</a> | Did he watch Kip on MythVision with Josh critiquing one of his Q&A videos, and what did he think? | He said they quote-mined and straw-manned an informal livestream answer, especially by expecting him to cover issues he was not addressing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=2839" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:19</a> | What did he think of the recent CT scan of King Amenhotep I? | He thought it was excellent technology, comparing it to virtual unrolling of scrolls and saying it could preserve artifacts while revealing hidden text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=2990" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">49:50</a> | What input or resources did he have for young-earth and old-earth questions in a church apologetics team? | He did not recommend specific resources, said he has concerns with every major origins position, and noted both scientific and exegetical reservations. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=3182" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:02</a> | Why are pyramids more famous ancient structures than ziggurats? | He said pyramids are larger, more visually striking, have internal and surrounding structures, and capture the imagination more than mud-brick ziggurats. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=3655" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:55</a> | How did the Israelites understand prophets, given passages like 1 Kings 22 with a class of prophets not necessarily sent by God? | He distinguished professional prophetic schools from prophets uniquely called by God, while noting God could still use someone trained in a prophetic school. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=3935" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:35</a> | Are there good Orthodox Jewish scholars on the Exodus and conquest of Canaan? | He recommended Joshua Berman as an excellent Orthodox Jewish scholar, including on the Exodus, conquest, and documentary hypothesis. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=4105" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:25</a> | What should a theology or history student read next after reading his book? | He mentioned the Five Views on the Exodus volume, Paul J. Lawrence's Egg Whites or Turnips, and Behind the Scenes of the Old Testament. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=4649" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:29</a> | Were Greeks inhabiting or trading with Egyptians before the Hellenization of Egypt? | Yes. He cited Naukratis in the seventh century BC and broader earlier trade evidence, including scarabs and possible Ramesses III-era contacts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=4928" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:08</a> | In Numbers 31:17, why were the males not spared along with the women who tempted Israel during the war with Midian? | He framed it in terms of ancient vendetta and blood revenge, saying the passage is about eliminating both the sin and future retaliation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=5285" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:28:05</a> | Who are his top five favorite Egyptologists, and what are favorite works by them? | He named figures including Kenneth Kitchen, Mark Lehner, Barry Kemp, David Wilkinson, and Manfred Bietak, highlighting Kitchen's Ramesside inscriptions and Bietak's work at Avaris. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=5478" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:31:18</a> | Was he planning a November 2022 video for the hundredth anniversary of Tutankhamun's tomb discovery? | He had not been planning one, but thought it was an excellent idea because the discovery changed Egyptology, archaeology, and biblical archaeology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=5578" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:58</a> | Could the Torah have originally been written in Egyptian, possibly hieroglyphs, and later translated into Hebrew? | He thought not, because the Torah's syntax looks Semitic rather than Egyptian, though it contains many Egyptian words. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=5898" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:18</a> | How did teenagers act and behave in the ancient Near East? | He said teenagers were treated more like adults, with adult responsibilities, though Egyptian society still distinguished childhood from later stages of life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=6190" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:43:10</a> | Is there anything in Egyptology that sheds light on the lion-men of Moab in 2 Samuel 23:20? | He saw a possible royal-inscription parallel in lion imagery for kings and warriors, but treated it cautiously rather than as an exact Egyptian match. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=6525" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:48:45</a> | Has he ever tried to write a fictional story or novel? | Yes. He said he has written several novels, enjoys fiction as a creative outlet, and hopes someday to write a good one. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=6689" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:29</a> | Did the Egyptians have an eschatology? | He said they had an afterlife eschatology but not an end-of-days one; they expected the world to continue eternally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=6870" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:54:30</a> | In Genesis 46:34, was shepherding an occupation associated with Levantines, and was Egyptian dislike of shepherds an example of xenophobia? | He said yes: Egyptians associated sheep and goat herding with Levantine Semites, preferred cattle, and showed broader xenophobia toward Levantines. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=7030" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:10</a> | What are the earliest ancient Egyptian archaeological finds outside the ancient Near East? | He said the answer is difficult, but pointed to scattered scarabs, Hittite correspondence from Ramesses II in Turkey, and only limited material beyond the Near East. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/KCxkCQlDTrY?t=7326" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:02:06</a> | How does knowing that a story follows an ancient pattern, such as patriarch stories following ancestor-epic patterns, affect interpretation? | He said patterns can help readers understand texts on ancient terms, but they do not by themselves prove fiction, borrowing, or any single conclusion. |
